cards.hook: The "Godfather of NDEs" built a rational case for the afterlife
cards.caption: The "Godfather of NDEs" built a rational worldview from near-death experiences, science, and philosophy. Fringe, or frontier? Weird-but-rigorous is exactly the stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: The line between fringe metaphysics and real science is thinner than academics admit.

cards.hook: Locke: kids learn more from a child's questions than a scholar's lectures
cards.caption: Locke argued a curious child's questions teach more than educated men's borrowed opinions. Writing like this now gets called anti-intellectual. Old ideas, freshly dangerous. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: We started mistaking credentials for thinking, and Locke saw it coming.

cards.hook: "What will you do with a philosophy degree?" Here's the savage answer
cards.caption: Someone's reply to "what'll you do with a philosophy degree": provide a veneer of intellectual respectability to a nasty hate movement. Brutal, funny, true. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Philosophy's real job market is laundering bad ideas into respectable ones.

cards.hook: Reason or revelation, pick one. Just don't ask me to merge them.
cards.caption: Reason or revelation, pick either. Just don't expect anyone to reconcile them for you. The cleanest take on faith vs logic I've seen all week. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: The demand to reconcile faith and reason is the trap, not the goal.

cards.hook: Analytic philosophy is the one field with no norm against name-calling
cards.caption: Fodor's dig: analytic philosophy is famously the one field with no norm against calling each other names. Insider shade that actually lands. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: The most rigorous field in the humanities is also the rudest, and it's proud of it.

cards.hook: Want to understand AI minds? Start with animal minds.
cards.caption: Want to understand AI minds? Start with animal minds. A philosophy-of-mind syllabus that runs from behaviourism to consciousness. The heady stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: You can't reason about machine consciousness without first reasoning about the animal kind.

cards.hook: 2,000 years of Chinese political thought in one chart.
cards.caption: 2,000 years of Chinese political thought in one hand-drawn chart. Daoism, Legalism, and every confusion you had, cleared up. Found before the timeline buried it.

cards.hook: The entire history of philosophy of mind, in one paragraph.
cards.caption: The entire history of philosophy of mind, in one paragraph: dualism, behaviourism, functionalism, then consciousness breaks everything. The end. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Every theory of mind eventually crashes into the hard problem and stops.

cards.hook: AI companies are quietly buying the philosophy of AI.
cards.caption: AI companies are quietly buying the philosophy of AI, and the field has zero rules on disclosing it. A petition wants that fixed. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: Philosophy is the one discipline with no conflict-of-interest standards, right as AI money floods in.

cards.hook: AI money is capturing the philosophers who judge AI.
cards.caption: AI money is capturing the philosophers who judge it. Same open letter, worth signing. The stuff my feed keeps.

cards.hook: Is language innate or learned? Stanford just settled the state of the debate.
cards.caption: Is language innate or learned? A fresh Stanford Encyclopedia entry maps the whole debate, and it hits different in the LLM era. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: The innateness question stopped being abstract the second LLMs learned grammar from raw text.

cards.hook: "Thinking is for poor people." This is where AI is taking us.
cards.caption: "I don't know if anyone needs to think anymore. It's AI now, thinking is for poor people." Cognitive surrender, sold as convenience. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Outsourcing your thinking to AI isn't efficiency, it's cognitive surrender dressed up as convenience.

cards.hook: You're a symphony of a trillion chemical reactions.
cards.caption: You're a symphony of a trillion chemical reactions, writing your life in symbols older than the Bronze Age. Consciousness never stops being strange. The kind of line my feed keeps.
cards.stance: "Just chemistry" isn't a downgrade of the mind — it's the most staggering thing about it.

cards.hook: "I think I think too much."
cards.caption: "I think I think too much." Six words that name the overthinker's trap: the mind auditing itself into paralysis. Small line, deep cut. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Overthinking isn't deep thinking — it's thinking that never ships.

cards.hook: Monty Python turned philosophy into a football match.
cards.caption: Monty Python put Socrates, Kant and Nietzsche on a football pitch — and it teaches more epistemology than most 101 lectures. Comedy as the best gateway drug to philosophy. Found before the timeline buried it.
cards.stance: The funniest take on philosophy is also one of the most accurate.

cards.hook: "Reason is immortal, all else mortal." — Pythagoras
cards.caption: "Reason is immortal, all else mortal." Pythagoras bet logic outlives the body — the same wager behind every mind-independent math argument today. 2,500 years, still unsettled. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Do logical truths exist without minds? Pythagoras already picked a side.

cards.hook: Reading Kant with no internet = an ogre reading Ulysses.
cards.caption: Someone nailed pre-internet Kant: an ogre grinding through Ulysses, catching the surface, missing "remorse of conscience" entirely. We forget how hard dense texts were before explainers existed. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: The internet didn't make us dumber — it made the hardest books finally readable.

cards.hook: Is agnosticism a moral duty — or an intellectual cop-out?
cards.caption: Is agnosticism a rigorous moral duty or a cop-out? A philosopher and scientist go 21 minutes on it — Huxley's survival tactic, Russell's teapot, the ethics of permanent doubt. The kind of debate my feed keeps.
cards.stance: "I don't know" is either the most honest position or the laziest one — pick.

cards.hook: Voltaire wrote "illusion" — but did he mean "delusion"?
cards.caption: One reader argues Voltaire's "the first of all pleasures" makes more sense as delusion, not illusion — and that word choice flips the whole meaning. Translation is philosophy in miniature. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: One mistranslated word can quietly rewrite a thinker's entire point.

cards.hook: De Beauvoir built an ethics with no rulebook
cards.caption: De Beauvoir built an ethics with no rulebook — freedom without a safety net. If existentialism ever felt cold, this is the human read. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: Existentialism isn't nihilism — it's the most demanding ethics there is.

cards.hook: Philosophy isn't science in a lab coat
cards.caption: Philosophy isn't just "soft science" — Mary Midgley says treating it that way guts what it's for. If you think STEM ate everything, read her. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: Reducing philosophy to a science department is how a university loses its mind.

cards.hook: If AI writes our papers, philosophy gets better
cards.caption: Hot take: if AI takes over citation-stuffed research, philosophy goes back to actually thinking hard — like the 1950s. Fewer footnotes, more ideas. The stuff my feed keeps.
cards.stance: AI killing literature-review busywork could save real philosophy.

cards.hook: Why care about a fictional character's fate?
cards.caption: "If Anna Karenina is fictional, why care what happens to her?" The honest answer: because you're not a heartless asshole. Best philosophy exam answer ever. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: Caring about fiction isn't irrational — it's the whole point of being human.

cards.hook: The women thinkers your philosophy class skipped
cards.caption: Open-access syllabus on women philosophers of the ancient and medieval world — the canon your class quietly skipped. Real gap, real fix. The stuff my feed keeps.

cards.hook: "Mathematics is an opinion — and the wrong one"
cards.caption: A philosopher argues math is an opinion, and the wrong one. Infuriating? Good — now try to prove him wrong. This is the fight platonists have dodged for centuries. Found before the timeline.
cards.stance: Math might be invented, not discovered — and that should scare you a little.
